title: Hand on Heart: Naming What's There
subtitle: A short body-based meditation for acknowledging the day's feelings before sleep
excerpt: A five-minute meditation for Day 6 of the sleep arc. Hand on heart, you name each feeling from the day without trying to fix or release it — simply acknowledging what was there before putting it down.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 6 theme of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025, ISBN 9798269649795). Hand-on-heart self-compassion gesture draws on Kristin Neff's research into self-compassion practice (2011 onward).

practice.whenToUse: When you arrive at bedtime with feelings still in the body from the day — use this sitting up before getting into bed.

content.text: A short sitting meditation for Day 6. Use it before bed rather than in bed — sitting up, back supported, eyes closed or soft. The practice names the day's feelings without trying to change them.
Sit with your back supported. Place one hand over your heart — flat, warm against your chest. Close your eyes. Take three slow breaths.
With your hand on your heart, ask yourself quietly: what am I holding right now?

Whatever comes up — name it. Not "I feel anxious about the conversation" but just: "anxious." Not "I'm tired of feeling this way" but just: "tired." One word at a time. Let each one land without trying to fix it or move past it too fast.
For each feeling you name, notice where it sits in the body. Not to analyse it — just to locate it. Chest, throat, jaw, stomach.
After each one, say inwardly: "I see you. You were here today." That's all it needs.
Continue until nothing new comes. Then sit quietly for a few breaths with your hand still on your heart.
Take a slow breath in, then let it go completely. Open your eyes when you're ready. The day can be done now.
Original to homemade.education. Built from the Day 6 theme of

(Rebecca J Page, 2025). The hand-on-heart gesture draws on Kristin Neff's research into self-compassion practice (2011 onward); the naming practice draws on mindfulness-based emotion labelling research.
